Activity 1 — Run the Investigation
1
2
3
Case 1 of 3
A cleaning pro disputes a 1-star review
The customer left a photo with their review. The pro says the photo doesn't show their work. Work through all four steps before making your decision.

Review Evidence

REVIEW PHOTO
📎 gemini_room_clean.jpg
Pro's dispute: "This is not a photo of my work. I have never cleaned a room that looks like this. I want this review removed."
1
Visual Inspection
Look for unnatural textures, anatomical errors, or logical inconsistencies
⌄
You're visually inspecting this image. What stands out?
✓ Right. Door hinges can only be on one side of a frame — having them on both is a logical impossibility a camera can't produce. This is a strong visible signal. Note it specifically when you write the Manager Comment.
Look at the door frame closely. Hinges appear on both sides — that's physically impossible in a real room. AI image generators frequently produce this kind of structural error. Low resolution and good staging alone are not signals.
2
Check the Filename
Look for keywords: "gemini," "chatgpt," "grok," "dall-e" — do not rename the file
⌄
Filename: gemini_room_clean.jpg
What does this filename tell you?
✓ Correct. The four keywords to watch for are: gemini, chatgpt, grok, dall-e. Finding one in the filename is a positive signal. The file must not be renamed — it is evidence.
"gemini" is one of four specific keywords associated with AI image generators. Phone cameras name files with dates and sequence numbers, not tool names. When you see gemini, chatgpt, grok, or dall-e in a filename, that is a signal — and the file must not be renamed.
3
Verify Metadata
Download the image and check its digital history
⌄

⬇ Try it yourself

Download this image and check its metadata on your own machine. The file will save as gemini_room_clean.jpg.

Mac
Windows
1. Click Download below
2. Open Finder — click the smiley face icon in your Dock, or press Command + Space and type "Finder"
3. Go to your Downloads folder in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Get Info
5. Scroll down and expand the More Info section
1. Click Download below
2. Open File Explorer — press Windows key + E
3. Click Downloads in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Properties
5. Click the Details tab
Download gemini_room_clean.jpg
Note: When you check Get Info on this image, More Info may show little or nothing — that's how most AI-generated images behave. The origin URL doesn't always appear. What you just practiced is the real workflow specialists use in the queue. The example below shows what a flagged image looks like when the URL is present, so you can recognize it when it shows up.

Example of what a flagged image looks like:

Mac — Get Info
Windows — Properties
FINDER → RIGHT-CLICK → GET INFO → MORE INFO
Where from: https://gemini.google.com/
Last opened: Today, 9:14 AM
Dimensions: 1024 × 1536
Color space: RGB
Alpha channel: No
↑ No camera hardware fields. Real photos show Camera make and Camera model here.
RIGHT-CLICK → PROPERTIES → DETAILS
Origin URL: https://gemini.google.com/
Date taken: —
Camera maker: —
Camera model: —
Dimensions: 1024 × 1536
↑ No camera make or model. Real photos populate these fields.
Based on the example above, what are the red flags in this metadata?
✓ Correct. Two red flags: an AI tool URL in "Where from," and the complete absence of camera hardware fields. Both indicate this image did not come from a real camera.
The two things to look for are: an AI tool URL in "Where from" (or Origin URL on Windows), and missing camera hardware fields. A missing creation date or unusual dimensions alone are not signals — real photos show Camera make and Camera model, and AI-generated images do not.
4
Technical Tools
Use Gemini (SynthID) or Chat GPT (C2PA) to confirm
⌄

Open Gemini or Chat GPT, upload the image, and ask whether it is AI-generated. Below is an example of what Gemini returned when this image was uploaded.

EXAMPLE — GEMINI RESPONSE

The SynthID identification tool didn't find evidence that this image was created with Google's AI tools. However, that doesn't rule out generation by a different AI model. Looking at the image:

  • The door has six hinges (three on each side) — highly unusual and non-functional for a standard interior door
  • Books on the shelves appear to have distorted or nonsensical textures rather than readable titles
  • The scene has the clean, uniform lighting and smooth surfaces characteristic of AI-generated interiors
  • The filename suggests it may have been generated using an AI image generator

While I can't say for certain, these details strongly suggest the image was created using an AI image generator.

What does this tool result mean for your decision?
✓ Correct. A tool doesn't need to find a SynthID watermark to confirm AI generation. When all four steps point in the same direction — visual inconsistency, AI filename, metadata without camera hardware, and a tool response identifying multiple AI signals — that is a confirmed violation.
The absence of a SynthID watermark doesn't clear the image. The tool still identified multiple strong visual signals and noted the filename as a further indicator. When every step of the investigation points to AI generation, the overall picture is what matters — not whether one specific watermark was found.

Make your decision

You've completed all four steps. What is your decision?

✓ Correct — Remove

Case 1 complete

Every step produced evidence pointing in the same direction. When the filename, metadata, visual signals, and tool response all align, the decision is clear.

In your Manager Comment, name the specific signals — don't just write "image appears fake." The door hinges, the filename, and the metadata are all concrete and documentable.

Signals found

👁 Door hinges on both sides of the frame — logical impossibility
📄 Filename contains "gemini" — AI generation keyword
🔍 Metadata: AI tool URL in "Where from," no camera hardware fields
🛠 Tool: multiple visual AI signals identified, filename flagged
Case 2 of 3
A pro submits text messages as dispute evidence
The pro attached a screenshot of a text exchange to support their dispute. Work through all four steps before deciding.

Review Evidence

DISPUTE ATTACHMENT
📎 screenshot_texts.jpg
⬇ Download to view full size
Pro's dispute: "The customer told me over text that the job was done well. Here is the proof. This review is false and retaliatory — I want it removed."
1
Visual Inspection
Look for unnatural textures, anatomical errors, or logical inconsistencies
⌄
You're visually inspecting this screenshot. What do you notice?
✓ Correct. In a genuine iOS text thread, font weight is consistent within each bubble and timestamps follow a fixed format. Variation in either is a visible signal of alteration. Name both specifically in the Manager Comment.
The content of the conversation and the iOS version are not signals. Look at the typography within the bubbles — font weight shifts mid-message — and the timestamps, which don't follow standard iOS formatting. Those are the visible signals here.
2
Check the Filename
Look for keywords: "gemini," "chatgpt," "grok," "dall-e"
⌄
Filename: screenshot_texts.jpg
What does this filename tell you?
✓ Correct. "Screenshot" is not one of the four keywords. Generic or descriptive filenames produce no signal from this step. Move to Step 3.
The four keywords are: gemini, chatgpt, grok, dall-e. "Screenshot" is not one of them, and phone naming conventions vary widely. Neither observation is a signal. When none of the four keywords are present, this step produces nothing — move to Step 3.
3
Verify Metadata
Download the image and check its digital history
⌄

⬇ Try it yourself

Download and check the metadata. Pay attention to what's there — and what isn't.

Mac
Windows
1. Click Download below
2. Open Finder — click the smiley face in your Dock, or press Command + Space and type "Finder"
3. Go to your Downloads folder in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Get Info
5. Scroll down and expand the More Info section
1. Click Download below
2. Open File Explorer — press Windows key + E
3. Click Downloads in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Properties
5. Click the Details tab
Download screenshot_texts.jpg

What you'll see on this screenshot:

Mac — Get Info
Windows — Properties
FINDER → RIGHT-CLICK → GET INFO → MORE INFO
Where from: —
Last opened: Today, 11:32 AM
Dimensions: 390 × 844
Color space: RGB
Alpha channel: No
RIGHT-CLICK → PROPERTIES → DETAILS
Origin URL: —
Date taken: —
Dimensions: 390 × 844
Camera maker: —
Camera model: —
The metadata is empty. What does that tell you?
✓ Correct. Screenshots strip all metadata — no origin URL, no camera hardware, nothing. That's not a red flag, and it's not a clean bill of health. It simply means this step can't tell you anything. The work happened in Step 1.
Empty metadata on a screenshot is completely normal — screenshots don't embed origin URLs or camera hardware data. Missing camera fields only flag as a signal on images that should have camera data, like claimed photos. On a screenshot, there's nothing to find here either way.
4
Technical Tools
Use Gemini (SynthID) or Chat GPT (C2PA) to confirm
⌄

Open Gemini or Chat GPT and upload the image. Below is the kind of result you'd receive on a screenshot.

EXAMPLE — TOOL RESULT ON A SCREENSHOT

No SynthID watermark detected.

No C2PA metadata found.

Note: This appears to be a screenshot. Screenshots do not carry embedded watermarks or C2PA credentials — tool results on screenshots are not conclusive.

Steps 3 and 4 produced nothing. Does that change your decision?
✓ Correct. Screenshots are specifically designed to bypass these technical checks — that's why the note exists. Empty results from Steps 3 and 4 on a screenshot mean nothing. Your evidence is in Step 1. Document the visible signals and remove.
Screenshots strip metadata and credentials — that's not a gap in the evidence, it's how screenshots work. Steps 3 and 4 will always come up empty on a screenshot. The investigation lives in Step 1, and those visible signals are sufficient to act on.

Make your decision

Steps 2, 3, and 4 produced no technical evidence. Step 1 produced visible signals. What is your decision?

✓ Correct — Remove

Case 2 complete

Screenshots are designed to bypass technical checks — that's expected. When Steps 3 and 4 produce nothing on a screenshot, that's not a gap in your case. Your evidence lives in Step 1.

Document the specific visible signals. "Image appears fake" is not enough — name the font weight inconsistency and the timestamp formatting in your Manager Comment.

Signals found

👁 Inconsistent font weight within message bubbles
👁 Timestamp formatting inconsistent with standard iOS
📄 Filename — no AI keyword (no signal)
🔍 Metadata — empty because it is a screenshot (expected, no signal)
🛠 Tools — no result because screenshots strip credentials (expected, no signal)
Case 3 of 3
A lawn care pro disputes a glowing review photo
The pro says they never worked on a lawn that looks like this. The photo was attached to a 5-star review. Work through all four steps before deciding.

Review Evidence

REVIEW PHOTO
📎 yard_photo_after.jpg
Pro's dispute: "This is not my work. I don't recognize this property at all. I want this photo investigated before it stays on my profile."
1
Visual Inspection
Look for unnatural textures, anatomical errors, or logical inconsistencies
⌄
You're visually inspecting this photo. What do you notice?
✓ Correct. With a single light source, all shadows fall in the same direction. Two shadows going opposite ways in the same scene cannot happen in a real photograph. That's the kind of logical inconsistency to look for and document specifically.
Perfectly manicured lawns and bright lighting are not signals — real professionals produce clean results and outdoor light varies. Look at the shadow directions: the tree shadow and the lamp post shadow fall in opposite directions. That is physically impossible with one light source.
2
Check the Filename
Look for keywords: "gemini," "chatgpt," "grok," "dall-e"
⌄
Filename: yard_photo_after.jpg
What does this filename tell you?
✓ Correct. Neither "after" nor any other part of this filename is one of the four keywords. Whether the naming pattern seems natural or unusual is not a factor — only the four keywords matter here. Move to Step 3.
A generic or renamed filename is not a signal on its own. Only the presence of gemini, chatgpt, grok, or dall-e matters in this step. None of those appear here. Move to Step 3.
3
Verify Metadata
Download the image and check its digital history
⌄

⬇ Try it yourself

Download and check the metadata. Notice what's here compared to Case 1.

Mac
Windows
1. Click Download below
2. Open Finder — click the smiley face in your Dock, or press Command + Space and type "Finder"
3. Go to your Downloads folder in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Get Info
5. Scroll down and expand the More Info section
1. Click Download below
2. Open File Explorer — press Windows key + E
3. Click Downloads in the left sidebar
4. Right-click the file → Properties
5. Click the Details tab
Download yard_photo_after.jpg

What you'll see on this image:

Mac — Get Info
Windows — Properties
FINDER → RIGHT-CLICK → GET INFO → MORE INFO
Where from: —
Last opened: Yesterday, 3:44 PM
Dimensions: 4032 × 3024
Color space: RGB
Color profile: Display P3
Camera make: Apple
Camera model: iPhone 14
Focal length: 5.1 mm
RIGHT-CLICK → PROPERTIES → DETAILS
Origin URL: —
Camera maker: Apple
Camera model: iPhone 14
Date taken: Yesterday, 3:44 PM
Dimensions: 4032 × 3024
What does this metadata tell you?
✓ Correct. Real camera hardware and no AI URL means this step produces nothing suspicious. But metadata alone can't confirm an image is genuine — it confirms there are no metadata red flags. The shadow inconsistency from Step 1 still stands.
Camera hardware data means this step produces no red flags — but it does not confirm the image is real. Metadata can be present on a manipulated photo. A blank "Where from" on a real photo is also completely normal. The signal from Step 1 is unaffected by what the metadata shows.
4
Technical Tools
Use Gemini (SynthID) or Chat GPT (C2PA) to confirm
⌄

Open Gemini or Chat GPT and upload the image. Below is the kind of result you'd receive on this photo.

EXAMPLE — TOOL RESULT

No SynthID watermark detected.

No C2PA metadata found.

The image appears to show a residential lawn. No specific AI generation indicators were identified through technical analysis.

Every technical step came back clean. You have one visible signal from Step 1. What do you do?
✓ Correct. Not every case produces technical evidence — and that's expected. When the investigation as a whole strongly suggests the image is fake, you act on that judgment. The shadow inconsistency is not a subjective impression — it is a physical impossibility. Document it precisely and remove.
Clean technical results don't override strong visual evidence — they just mean the technical steps produced nothing. The shadow inconsistency is not a borderline observation; it is something that cannot happen in a real photograph. When your investigation strongly points to a fake, that's what you act on.

Make your decision

One strong visible signal. Steps 2, 3, and 4 produced no technical evidence. What is your decision?

✓ Correct — Remove

Case 3 complete

This is the hardest case type. Clean technical results can make a strong visual signal feel less certain — but they don't cancel it. The shadow inconsistency is something a real photograph cannot produce.

Document the specific signal in your Manager Comment. "Image appears fake" is not enough. Name what you saw and why it's physically impossible.

Signals found

👁 Shadows fall in two different directions — physically impossible in a real photograph
📄 Filename — no AI keyword (no signal)
🔍 Metadata — real iPhone 14 hardware, no AI URL (no signal)
🛠 Tools — no watermark or C2PA found (no signal)
✅

Investigation complete

You've worked through all three cases. The four-step process applies every time — even when most steps produce nothing.
